1	# Tasks: rerun-after-halt-detect-cleanly
2	
3	Design doc: ../design-decisions.md (§ "Out of scope" — retry is explicitly operator-owned, so this work is detect-and-emit-halt-UX, not auto-cleanup)
4	Linear issue: [ABA-231 — git worktree add fails opaquely when rerunning a halted cycle](https://linear.app/ababushkin/issue/ABA-231); ops-slot work (no parent user story; US-B is closed)
5	Last updated: 2026-05-22
6	
7	## Surfaced via
8	
9	Whole-codebase `/code-review-and-quality` audit on 2026-05-22 (post-commit `13d9f70`). Two findings filed; #1 (`first_pending_issue` dead code) landed as commit `83876f7`. This file plans finding #2.
10	
11	The review's verbatim framing: on halt, drain-cycle preserves `.worktrees/<id>/` and branch `<id>` for inspection (intentional, US-B). On rerun, the orchestrator re-picks the same first issue and calls `git worktree add -b <id> ...` which fails because both exist; `subprocess.run(check=True)` raises `CalledProcessError` and the operator gets a Python traceback rather than the spec-shaped `Halt: ...` UX they were trained to expect from US-B.
12	
13	## Confirmed inputs
14	
15	- **US-B is closed.** ABA-195 / Task 3 (ABA-214) shipped the live acceptance smoke on 2026-05-22. This work is a post-acceptance follow-up, not a US-B re-open. `rules/linear-workflow.md` classifies it as ops-slot work (< 5 issues, not goal-shaped).
16	- **`_halt_message` helper is in place.** `drain_cycle/orchestrator.py` already has the single-source-of-truth halt-line helper used by both stderr `print` and `runlog.append_entry(halt_reason=...)` (ABA-212/213). The new branch extends/reuses it rather than parallel-stringing the format.
17	- **Run-log is per-invocation.** Since ABA-230 (commit `bb4fd91`), each invocation writes its own `<cycle-id>-<run-timestamp>.json`, so the rerun-detect-halt entry will land in a *new* file, not clobber the prior halt's file. Test must glob `~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>-*.json` rather than expect a deterministic filename.
18	- **No auto-cleanup.** `docs/design-decisions.md` §"Out of scope" pins this: "A halted issue is not retried automatically. The operator inspects the worktree and decides — fix, redo manually, or descope." Detect-and-fail-cleanly preserves that contract; the preserved worktree dir and branch are left exactly as they were on the prior halt.
19	- **Detection must cover both halves.** Either the worktree dir or the branch can be present alone after a partial manual cleanup. The pre-check must flag *either* condition, not require both.
20	
21	## Design decision pinned upfront
22	
23	**Does the rerun-detect-and-halt case produce a run-log entry?** → **Yes — Option A.**
24	
25	Rationale: today's invariant is "one entry per attempted issue" and downstream KR1 grading reads `final_linear_state` per entry. If the operator reruns a halted cycle three times without manual cleanup, all three attempts should be visible in the run-log so KR1 sees the repeated-halt pattern. Option B (stderr-only) would make repeat-halt invisible to grading.
26	
27	Cost of Option A: ~6 lines of orchestrator code to construct the entry with cached `issue["state"]["name"]`, 0-second duration, and an `exit_code` sentinel for the no-spawn case (see Open Questions for sentinel choice).
28	
29	## Task list
30	
31	### Task 1 — Detect prior-halt artefacts and emit spec-shaped halt + run-log entry
32	**Linear:** [ABA-231](https://linear.app/ababushkin/issue/ABA-231) (ops-slot, no parent user story)
33	**Description:** Add a pre-check to `drain_cycle/worktree.add()` that raises a new exception (e.g. `PriorArtefactsExist`) carrying the worktree path *before* invoking `git worktree add`, when *either* `.worktrees/<id>/` exists or branch `<id>` exists. `drain_cycle/orchestrator.run()` catches it before calling `linear.set_state(..., "In Progress")` (the orchestrator must not transition the issue when no spawn will follow), constructs a halt line via the existing `_halt_message` helper (extended to a sibling variant or with an optional reason argument — same `Halt:` token prefix so the operator's grep anchor remains the single scannable line), writes a run-log entry with `issue_identifier`, cached `issue["state"]["name"]` as `final_linear_state`, `started_at == finished_at` (0-second duration), `exit_code` sentinel for the no-spawn case, and `halt_reason` equal to the stderr line, prints the halt line on stderr, and returns 1.
34	**Done when:** a new integration test (new file `tests/test_orchestrator_rerun_halt.py` or extension of `tests/test_orchestrator_halt.py`) asserts:
35	  (a) the orchestrator process exits non-zero;
36	  (b) exactly one stderr line begins with `Halt: ` and contains the issue identifier and the preserved worktree path;
37	  (c) globbing `~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>-*.json` (per ABA-230's per-run filename format) returns exactly one file, whose payload has exactly one entry for the picked issue, with `halt_reason` equal to the stderr line, `final_linear_state` equal to the cached `Todo` state, and `cycle_duration_seconds == 0.0`;
38	  (d) `linear.set_state` was NOT called for the picked issue (no Todo→In Progress transition on the no-spawn branch);
39	  (e) the second issue in the cycle was not touched (no worktree created, no `set_state`, no run-log entry);
40	  (f) the pre-existing worktree dir and branch are left in place (no auto-cleanup);
41	  (g) full `pytest` remains green;
42	  AND parametrising the test for the two pre-seeded states proves both halves of the detector:
43	    (g1) worktree dir present + branch present (the post-halt steady state);
44	    (g2) worktree dir present + branch absent (operator partial cleanup);
45	    (g3) worktree dir absent + branch present (operator partial cleanup).
46	**Dependencies:** none. US-B's machinery (`_halt_message`, the `halt_reason` field on run-log entries, the `Halt:` stderr-line shape) is already in place. No coupling to in-flight work.
47	
48	## Out of scope (deliberate)
49	
50	- **Auto-cleanup of preserved worktrees or branches.** Operator owns retry per `docs/design-decisions.md`.
51	- **Changes to the agent prompt.** Spawn-time contract is unaffected; this branch fires before any spawn.
52	- **Changes to the agent-self-updates-Linear contract.** No Linear state transition happens on the rerun-detect branch.
53	- **Recovery helper / drain-cycle subcommand.** A future "drain-cycle clean <id>" helper might be useful, but is not in this task.
54	
55	## Open questions
56	
57	- **Halt-line wording** for the rerun-detect case. Working text: `Halt: <id> (prior-halt artefacts at <worktree-path>) — clean up before retry`. The `Halt:` prefix + issue identifier + worktree path are the load-bearing invariants; exact prose is decided during implementation, no separate owner.
58	- **`exit_code` sentinel** in the run-log entry for the no-spawn case. Working choice: `-1`. Alternatives: `null` (requires `runlog` schema change to allow `int | None`, plus a docstring update), or `0` (collides with successful-Done semantics — rejected). Resolution: during implementation; the test's required-keys + per-field-type assertions in `tests/test_runlog.py` will catch any choice the current schema doesn't already allow.

# Tasks: rerun-after-halt-detect-cleanly

Design doc: ../design-decisions.md (§ "Out of scope" — retry is explicitly operator-owned, so this work is detect-and-emit-halt-UX, not auto-cleanup)
Linear issue: [ABA-231 — git worktree add fails opaquely when rerunning a halted cycle](https://linear.app/ababushkin/issue/ABA-231); ops-slot work (no parent user story; US-B is closed)
Last updated: 2026-05-22

## Surfaced via

Whole-codebase `/code-review-and-quality` audit on 2026-05-22 (post-commit `13d9f70`). Two findings filed; #1 (`first_pending_issue` dead code) landed as commit `83876f7`. This file plans finding #2.

The review's verbatim framing: on halt, drain-cycle preserves `.worktrees/<id>/` and branch `<id>` for inspection (intentional, US-B). On rerun, the orchestrator re-picks the same first issue and calls `git worktree add -b <id> ...` which fails because both exist; `subprocess.run(check=True)` raises `CalledProcessError` and the operator gets a Python traceback rather than the spec-shaped `Halt: ...` UX they were trained to expect from US-B.

## Confirmed inputs

- **US-B is closed.** ABA-195 / Task 3 (ABA-214) shipped the live acceptance smoke on 2026-05-22. This work is a post-acceptance follow-up, not a US-B re-open. `rules/linear-workflow.md` classifies it as ops-slot work (< 5 issues, not goal-shaped).
- **`_halt_message` helper is in place.** `drain_cycle/orchestrator.py` already has the single-source-of-truth halt-line helper used by both stderr `print` and `runlog.append_entry(halt_reason=...)` (ABA-212/213). The new branch extends/reuses it rather than parallel-stringing the format.
- **Run-log is per-invocation.** Since ABA-230 (commit `bb4fd91`), each invocation writes its own `<cycle-id>-<run-timestamp>.json`, so the rerun-detect-halt entry will land in a *new* file, not clobber the prior halt's file. Test must glob `~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>-*.json` rather than expect a deterministic filename.
- **No auto-cleanup.** `docs/design-decisions.md` §"Out of scope" pins this: "A halted issue is not retried automatically. The operator inspects the worktree and decides — fix, redo manually, or descope." Detect-and-fail-cleanly preserves that contract; the preserved worktree dir and branch are left exactly as they were on the prior halt.
- **Detection must cover both halves.** Either the worktree dir or the branch can be present alone after a partial manual cleanup. The pre-check must flag *either* condition, not require both.

## Design decision pinned upfront

**Does the rerun-detect-and-halt case produce a run-log entry?** → **Yes — Option A.**

Rationale: today's invariant is "one entry per attempted issue" and downstream KR1 grading reads `final_linear_state` per entry. If the operator reruns a halted cycle three times without manual cleanup, all three attempts should be visible in the run-log so KR1 sees the repeated-halt pattern. Option B (stderr-only) would make repeat-halt invisible to grading.

Cost of Option A: ~6 lines of orchestrator code to construct the entry with cached `issue["state"]["name"]`, 0-second duration, and an `exit_code` sentinel for the no-spawn case (see Open Questions for sentinel choice).

## Task list

### Task 1 — Detect prior-halt artefacts and emit spec-shaped halt + run-log entry
**Linear:** [ABA-231](https://linear.app/ababushkin/issue/ABA-231) (ops-slot, no parent user story)
**Description:** Add a pre-check to `drain_cycle/worktree.add()` that raises a new exception (e.g. `PriorArtefactsExist`) carrying the worktree path *before* invoking `git worktree add`, when *either* `.worktrees/<id>/` exists or branch `<id>` exists. `drain_cycle/orchestrator.run()` catches it before calling `linear.set_state(..., "In Progress")` (the orchestrator must not transition the issue when no spawn will follow), constructs a halt line via the existing `_halt_message` helper (extended to a sibling variant or with an optional reason argument — same `Halt:` token prefix so the operator's grep anchor remains the single scannable line), writes a run-log entry with `issue_identifier`, cached `issue["state"]["name"]` as `final_linear_state`, `started_at == finished_at` (0-second duration), `exit_code` sentinel for the no-spawn case, and `halt_reason` equal to the stderr line, prints the halt line on stderr, and returns 1.
**Done when:** a new integration test (new file `tests/test_orchestrator_rerun_halt.py` or extension of `tests/test_orchestrator_halt.py`) asserts:
  (a) the orchestrator process exits non-zero;
  (b) exactly one stderr line begins with `Halt: ` and contains the issue identifier and the preserved worktree path;
  (c) globbing `~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>-*.json` (per ABA-230's per-run filename format) returns exactly one file, whose payload has exactly one entry for the picked issue, with `halt_reason` equal to the stderr line, `final_linear_state` equal to the cached `Todo` state, and `cycle_duration_seconds == 0.0`;
  (d) `linear.set_state` was NOT called for the picked issue (no Todo→In Progress transition on the no-spawn branch);
  (e) the second issue in the cycle was not touched (no worktree created, no `set_state`, no run-log entry);
  (f) the pre-existing worktree dir and branch are left in place (no auto-cleanup);
  (g) full `pytest` remains green;
  AND parametrising the test for the two pre-seeded states proves both halves of the detector:
    (g1) worktree dir present + branch present (the post-halt steady state);
    (g2) worktree dir present + branch absent (operator partial cleanup);
    (g3) worktree dir absent + branch present (operator partial cleanup).
**Dependencies:** none. US-B's machinery (`_halt_message`, the `halt_reason` field on run-log entries, the `Halt:` stderr-line shape) is already in place. No coupling to in-flight work.

## Out of scope (deliberate)

- **Auto-cleanup of preserved worktrees or branches.** Operator owns retry per `docs/design-decisions.md`.
- **Changes to the agent prompt.** Spawn-time contract is unaffected; this branch fires before any spawn.
- **Changes to the agent-self-updates-Linear contract.** No Linear state transition happens on the rerun-detect branch.
- **Recovery helper / drain-cycle subcommand.** A future "drain-cycle clean <id>" helper might be useful, but is not in this task.

## Open questions

- **Halt-line wording** for the rerun-detect case. Working text: `Halt: <id> (prior-halt artefacts at <worktree-path>) — clean up before retry`. The `Halt:` prefix + issue identifier + worktree path are the load-bearing invariants; exact prose is decided during implementation, no separate owner.
- **`exit_code` sentinel** in the run-log entry for the no-spawn case. Working choice: `-1`. Alternatives: `null` (requires `runlog` schema change to allow `int | None`, plus a docstring update), or `0` (collides with successful-Done semantics — rejected). Resolution: during implementation; the test's required-keys + per-field-type assertions in `tests/test_runlog.py` will catch any choice the current schema doesn't already allow.

def _halt_message(identifier: str, state_name: str, worktree_path: Path) -> str:
    """Single source of truth for the halt UX.

    The same string lands on stderr (the operator's grep anchor) and in
    the run-log entry's ``halt_reason`` field — so kill-condition tooling

input.new_string:
def _halt_message(identifier: str, state_name: str, worktree_path: Path) -> str:
    """Single source of truth for the halt UX.

    The same string lands on stderr (the operator's grep anchor) and in
    the run-log entry's ``halt_reason`` field — so kill-condition tooling
    reads the same human-readable explanation the operator saw at halt
    time.
    """
    return f"Halt: {identifier} (final state: {state_name}) at {worktree_path}"


def _resume_attempts(cycle_id: str, identifier: str) -> int:
    """Count prior halted attempts for ``identifier`` across this cycle's run logs.

    Globs ``~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle_id>-*.json`` and tallies entries
    whose ``issue_identifier`` matches and whose ``final_linear_state``
    is not ``"Done"`` — the same shape every halt path writes. The
    orchestrator compares this against ``limits.max_resume_attempts``
    before spawning, so a perma-stuck issue stops consuming attempts
    once its budget is spent. Unreadable or malformed log files are
    skipped rather than failed: a partial-write file from a SIGKILL'd
    earlier run must not pin the operator out of running the cycle.
    """
    count = 0
    for path in runlog.runs_dir().glob(f"{cycle_id}-*.json"):
        try:
            payload = json.loads(path.read_text())
        except (OSError, json.JSONDecodeError):
            continue
        for entry in payload.get("entries", []):
            if (
                entry.get("issue_identifier") == identifier
                and entry.get("final_linear_state") != "Done"
            ):
                count += 1
    return count

def _halt_message(identifier: str, state_name: str, worktree_path: Path) -> str:
    """Single source of truth for the halt UX.

    The same string lands on stderr (the operator's grep anchor) and in
    the run-log entry's ``halt_reason`` field — so kill-condition tooling
    reads the same human-readable explanation the operator saw at halt
    time.
    """
    return f"Halt: {identifier} (final state: {state_name}) at {worktree_path}"
toolUseResult.newString:
def _halt_message(identifier: str, state_name: str, worktree_path: Path) -> str:
    """Single source of truth for the halt UX.

    The same string lands on stderr (the operator's grep anchor) and in
    the run-log entry's ``halt_reason`` field — so kill-condition tooling
    reads the same human-readable explanation the operator saw at halt
    time.
    """
    return f"Halt: {identifier} (final state: {state_name}) at {worktree_path}"


def _resume_attempts(cycle_id: str, identifier: str) -> int:
    """Count prior halted attempts for ``identifier`` across this cycle's run logs.

    Globs ``~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle_id>-*.json`` and tallies entries
    whose ``issue_identifier`` matches and whose ``final_linear_state``
    is not ``"Done"`` — the same shape every halt path writes. The
    orchestrator compares this against ``limits.max_resume_attempts``
    before spawning, so a perma-stuck issue stops consuming attempts
    once its budget is spent. Unreadable or malformed log files are
    skipped rather than failed: a partial-write file from a SIGKILL'd
    earlier run must not pin the operator out of running the cycle.
    """
    count = 0
    for path in runlog.runs_dir().glob(f"{cycle_id}-*.json"):
        try:
            payload = json.loads(path.read_text())
        except (OSError, json.JSONDecodeError):
            continue
        for entry in payload.get("entries", []):
            if (
                entry.get("issue_identifier") == identifier
                and entry.get("final_linear_state") != "Done"
            ):
                count += 1
    return count

content.text: Now writing the orchestrator-side resume tests.

input.content:
"""Orchestrator resume-on-re-run wiring.

These exercise the three orchestrator effects that turn a re-run after a
halt from a manual-cleanup chore into a clean continuation: ``worktree.ensure``
reuses a preserved worktree instead of failing on a leftover branch,
``prompt.build`` is called with ``resumed=True`` so the spawned agent
reads the live worktree before continuing, and the ``max_resume_attempts``
policy cap refuses to spawn once a perma-stuck issue has burned its budget.

Substitution mirrors ``test_orchestrator_halt.py``: real git repo, in-process
Linear stub via attribute monkey-patching, fake ``claude`` shell script as
``_CLAUDE_CMD``. The new bit is pre-seeding the run-log directory with halt
entries to drive ``_resume_attempts`` without first staging halts in this
process.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import json
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path

import pytest

from drain_cycle import limits, linear, orchestrator, prompt, repos


_TEST_REPO_NAME = "test-repo"
_CYCLE_ID = "stub-cycle-id"


def _issue(identifier: str, sort_order: float) -> dict:
    return {
        "id": f"id-{identifier}",
        "identifier": identifier,
        "title": f"Title for {identifier}",
        "description": f"Body for {identifier}",
        "sortOrder": sort_order,
        "state": {"type": "unstarted", "name": "Todo"},
        "labels": [f"repo:{_TEST_REPO_NAME}"],
    }


def _stub_repos(repo_path: Path) -> repos.Repos:
    return repos.Repos(mapping={_TEST_REPO_NAME: repo_path})


def _init_repo(repo: Path) -> None:
    subprocess.run(["git", "init", "-b", "main"], cwd=repo, check=True, capture_output=True)
    subprocess.run(
        ["git", "config", "user.email", "test@example.com"], cwd=repo, check=True
    )
    subprocess.run(["git", "config", "user.name", "Test"], cwd=repo, check=True)
    (repo / "README.md").write_text("seed\n")
    subprocess.run(["git", "add", "."], cwd=repo, check=True, capture_output=True)
    subprocess.run(
        ["git", "commit", "-m", "seed"], cwd=repo, check=True, capture_output=True
    )


def _noop_claude(tmp_path: Path) -> Path:
    """``claude -p`` stand-in that exits cleanly without flipping Linear.

    The orchestrator's not-Done branch then halts, which is the post-spawn
    shape these tests want — the worktree is preserved, the halt is
    recorded, and the next re-run drives the resume path.
    """
    script = tmp_path / "noop-claude.sh"
    script.write_text("#!/bin/sh\nexit 0\n")
    script.chmod(0o755)
    return script


def _seed_runlog_entry(
    runs_dir: Path,
    *,
    cycle_id: str,
    identifier: str,
    final_state: str,
    suffix: str,
) -> Path:
    """Write a minimal run-log file with one entry for ``identifier``.

    Mirrors the on-disk schema enough for ``_resume_attempts`` to read it:
    only ``cycle_id`` + ``entries[].issue_identifier`` + ``final_linear_state``
    are inspected. Filename uses ``<cycle_id>-<suffix>.json`` so the cycle's
    glob picks it up.
    """
    runs_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    path = runs_dir / f"{cycle_id}-{suffix}.json"
    path.write_text(
        json.dumps(
            {
                "cycle_id": cycle_id,
                "cycle_duration_seconds": 0.0,
                "cycle_cost_usd": 0.0,
                "cycle_tokens_cumulative": 0,
                "cycle_halt_reason": None,
                "entries": [
                    {
                        "issue_identifier": identifier,
                        "started_at": "2026-05-28T10:00:00+00:00",
                        "finished_at": "2026-05-28T10:01:00+00:00",
                        "exit_code": -1,
                        "final_linear_state": final_state,
                        "worktree_path": "/tmp/stub",
                        "halt_reason": (
                            None
                            if final_state == "Done"
                            else f"Halt: {identifier} (final state: {final_state})"
                        ),
                        "duration_seconds": 60.0,
                        "model": None,
                        "usage": None,
                        "cost_usd": None,
                        "num_turns": None,
                        "session_id": None,
                        "is_error": None,
                    }
                ],
            }
        )
        + "\n"
    )
    return path


def _patch_linear(
    monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch,
    raw_issues: list[dict],
    *,
    set_state_calls: list[tuple[str, str]],
) -> None:
    """Common Linear-side substitutions for these tests.

    ``current_cycle_id`` returns the fixed test cycle id, ``pending_issues``
    serves the planned order, and ``set_state`` records calls (so a test
    can assert it was — or was not — invoked). ``get_issue`` returns the
    original issue verbatim, which makes a no-op claude script look like
    a not-Done halt to the orchestrator.
    """
    issues_by_id = {i["id"]: i for i in raw_issues}

    def fake_set_state(issue_id: str, state_name: str) -> None:
        set_state_calls.append((issue_id, state_name))

    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "current_cycle_id", lambda: _CYCLE_ID)
    monkeypatch.setattr(
        linear, "pending_issues", lambda cycle_id: linear._plan(raw_issues)
    )
    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "set_state", fake_set_state)
    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "get_issue", lambda issue_id: issues_by_id[issue_id])


def test_resume_reuses_preserved_worktree_and_signals_resumed_prompt(
    tmp_path: Path, monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch
) -> None:
    """Pre-existing worktree on disk is reused; the spawned prompt carries
    ``resumed=True``.

    This is the headline resume contract: a re-run after a halt no longer
    fails on ``git worktree add`` (the previous failure mode), no longer
    requires manual cleanup, and the spawned agent learns it is continuing
    rather than starting fresh — both halves matter, so they are pinned
    in one test.
    """
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)
    monkeypatch.chdir(repo)
    monkeypatch.setenv("HOME", str(tmp_path))

    issue = _issue("ABA-STUCK", sort_order=1.0)

    # Pre-create the preserved worktree as if a prior run halted here.
    preserved = repo / ".worktrees" / issue["identifier"]
    subprocess.run(
        [
            "git",
            "worktree",
            "add",
            "-b",
            issue["identifier"],
            str(preserved),
            "main",
        ],
        cwd=repo,
        check=True,
        capture_output=True,
    )
    sentinel = preserved / "PRESERVED_FILE"
    sentinel.write_text("from the prior halt\n")

    # Pre-seed one halted entry so the orchestrator's resume-cap sees this
    # as a re-run, not a first attempt.
    runs_dir = tmp_path / ".drain-cycle" / "runs"
    _seed_runlog_entry(
        runs_dir,
        cycle_id=_CYCLE_ID,
        identifier=issue["identifier"],
        final_state="Todo",
        suffix="prior",
    )

    build_calls: list[dict] = []
    real_build = prompt.build

    def recording_build(issue_arg, worktree_arg, *, resumed=False):
        build_calls.append(
            {"identifier": issue_arg["identifier"], "resumed": resumed}
        )
        return real_build(issue_arg, worktree_arg, resumed=resumed)

    monkeypatch.setattr(prompt, "build", recording_build)

    set_state_calls: list[tuple[str, str]] = []
    _patch_linear(monkeypatch, [issue], set_state_calls=set_state_calls)

    monkeypatch.setattr(orchestrator, "_CLAUDE_CMD", [str(_noop_claude(tmp_path))])

    exit_code = orchestrator.run(_stub_repos(repo))
    # The no-op claude leaves Linear at Todo, so the orchestrator halts on
    # the not-Done branch — but the load-bearing assertions below are about
    # what happened BEFORE that halt: ensure reused, prompt was resumed.
    assert exit_code == 1

    # The orchestrator called prompt.build with resumed=True — the resume
    # directive will be in the spawned-agent prompt.
    assert build_calls == [{"identifier": issue["identifier"], "resumed": True}]

    # The preserved worktree's sentinel file is still there — ensure did
    # not blow it away or recreate the worktree (which would have wiped
    # the gitignored sentinel).
    assert sentinel.read_text() == "from the prior halt\n"

    # Pre-spawn set_state(In Progress) still fires on a resume — the
    # lifecycle half-owned by the orchestrator does not change because the
    # worktree was reused.
    assert (issue["id"], "In Progress") in set_state_calls


def test_resume_cap_halts_before_any_state_change(
    tmp_path: Path,
    monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch,
    capsys: pytest.CaptureFixture[str],
) -> None:
    """With prior halts at the cap, the orchestrator refuses to spawn.

    The refusal is a no-spawn halt: no ``set_state``, no worktree call,
    no ``claude`` invocation. The halt entry is written so KR1 grading
    sees the refused attempt, and the halt line names the cap so the
    operator knows how to clear it.
    """
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)
    monkeypatch.chdir(repo)
    monkeypatch.setenv("HOME", str(tmp_path))

    stuck = _issue("ABA-STUCK", sort_order=1.0)
    later = _issue("ABA-LATER", sort_order=2.0)

    # Seed two prior halted entries so an ``max_resume_attempts=2`` cap
    # has been reached.
    runs_dir = tmp_path / ".drain-cycle" / "runs"
    for suffix in ("first", "second"):
        _seed_runlog_entry(
            runs_dir,
            cycle_id=_CYCLE_ID,
            identifier=stuck["identifier"],
            final_state="Todo",
            suffix=suffix,
        )

    set_state_calls: list[tuple[str, str]] = []
    _patch_linear(monkeypatch, [stuck, later], set_state_calls=set_state_calls)

    # Claude must never be spawned on the cap-halt path; ``set_state``
    # must never be called either (the cap fires before either).
    forbidden = tmp_path / "forbidden-claude.sh"
    forbidden.write_text("#!/bin/sh\nexit 99\n")
    forbidden.chmod(0o755)
    monkeypatch.setattr(orchestrator, "_CLAUDE_CMD", [str(forbidden)])

    exit_code = orchestrator.run(
        _stub_repos(repo), limits.Limits(max_resume_attempts=2)
    )
    assert exit_code == 1

    # No set_state, no spawned worker. The cap-halt fires before either.
    assert set_state_calls == []
    assert not (repo / ".worktrees" / stuck["identifier"]).exists()

    # Single fresh run-log file (the current run) plus the two pre-seeded
    # files. The current one holds exactly one entry: the cap-halt.
    log_files = sorted(runs_dir.glob(f"{_CYCLE_ID}-*.json"))
    assert len(log_files) == 3  # two seeded + one current
    # The current run's file is the one not in the seeded suffixes.
    current_payload = next(
        json.loads(p.read_text())
        for p in log_files
        if p.stem not in (f"{_CYCLE_ID}-first", f"{_CYCLE_ID}-second")
    )
    assert len(current_payload["entries"]) == 1
    entry = current_payload["entries"][0]
    assert entry["issue_identifier"] == stuck["identifier"]
    assert entry["final_linear_state"] == stuck["state"]["name"]
    assert "resume-attempt cap reached (2/2)" in entry["halt_reason"]

    # Stderr halt line matches the run-log halt_reason exactly — the same
    # invariant every other halt path holds.
    stderr_lines = capsys.readouterr().err.splitlines()
    (halt_line,) = [line for line in stderr_lines if line.startswith("Halt: ")]
    assert entry["halt_reason"] == halt_line
    assert "max_resume_attempts" in halt_line

    # Second issue never attempted.
    assert all(
        e["issue_identifier"] != later["identifier"]
        for e in current_payload["entries"]
    )


def test_resume_cap_none_is_unbounded(
    tmp_path: Path, monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch
) -> None:
    """``max_resume_attempts=None`` lets a perma-stuck issue keep retrying.

    The operator opts into that risk explicitly by setting the cap to
    ``null`` in ``limits.yml``. Five prior halts must not block the
    sixth attempt.
    """
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)
    monkeypatch.chdir(repo)
    monkeypatch.setenv("HOME", str(tmp_path))

    issue = _issue("ABA-STUCK", sort_order=1.0)

    runs_dir = tmp_path / ".drain-cycle" / "runs"
    for i in range(5):
        _seed_runlog_entry(
            runs_dir,
            cycle_id=_CYCLE_ID,
            identifier=issue["identifier"],
            final_state="Todo",
            suffix=f"halt{i}",
        )

    set_state_calls: list[tuple[str, str]] = []
    _patch_linear(monkeypatch, [issue], set_state_calls=set_state_calls)

    monkeypatch.setattr(orchestrator, "_CLAUDE_CMD", [str(_noop_claude(tmp_path))])

    # max_resume_attempts=None — the cap is off, the orchestrator must
    # still attempt the issue (and the no-op claude then halts on not-Done
    # like any first attempt, which is fine for this test's purpose).
    exit_code = orchestrator.run(
        _stub_repos(repo), limits.Limits(max_resume_attempts=None)
    )
    assert exit_code == 1
    # The pre-spawn In Progress transition fired — proves the cap did NOT
    # short-circuit ahead of it.
    assert (issue["id"], "In Progress") in set_state_calls


def test_resume_cap_does_not_count_done_entries(
    tmp_path: Path, monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch
) -> None:
    """Prior Done entries are excluded from the cap tally.

    Without this, a successfully drained issue in a prior run would
    consume the resume budget of a later, unrelated re-run against the
    same cycle — pinning the cap to ``Done`` entries instead of halts.
    """
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)
    monkeypatch.chdir(repo)
    monkeypatch.setenv("HOME", str(tmp_path))

    issue = _issue("ABA-STUCK", sort_order=1.0)
    runs_dir = tmp_path / ".drain-cycle" / "runs"

    # Five prior Done entries plus one prior halt. With max_resume_attempts=2
    # the cap is NOT reached (1 < 2) — the run must proceed.
    for i in range(5):
        _seed_runlog_entry(
            runs_dir,
            cycle_id=_CYCLE_ID,
            identifier=issue["identifier"],
            final_state="Done",
            suffix=f"done{i}",
        )
    _seed_runlog_entry(
        runs_dir,
        cycle_id=_CYCLE_ID,
        identifier=issue["identifier"],
        final_state="Todo",
        suffix="halt",
    )

    set_state_calls: list[tuple[str, str]] = []
    _patch_linear(monkeypatch, [issue], set_state_calls=set_state_calls)

    monkeypatch.setattr(orchestrator, "_CLAUDE_CMD", [str(_noop_claude(tmp_path))])

    exit_code = orchestrator.run(
        _stub_repos(repo), limits.Limits(max_resume_attempts=2)
    )
    assert exit_code == 1
    # Pre-spawn transition fired — Done entries weren't counted.
    assert (issue["id"], "In Progress") in set_state_calls

toolUseResult.content:
"""Orchestrator resume-on-re-run wiring.

These exercise the three orchestrator effects that turn a re-run after a
halt from a manual-cleanup chore into a clean continuation: ``worktree.ensure``
reuses a preserved worktree instead of failing on a leftover branch,
``prompt.build`` is called with ``resumed=True`` so the spawned agent
reads the live worktree before continuing, and the ``max_resume_attempts``
policy cap refuses to spawn once a perma-stuck issue has burned its budget.

Substitution mirrors ``test_orchestrator_halt.py``: real git repo, in-process
Linear stub via attribute monkey-patching, fake ``claude`` shell script as
``_CLAUDE_CMD``. The new bit is pre-seeding the run-log directory with halt
entries to drive ``_resume_attempts`` without first staging halts in this
process.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import json
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path

import pytest

from drain_cycle import limits, linear, orchestrator, prompt, repos


_TEST_REPO_NAME = "test-repo"
_CYCLE_ID = "stub-cycle-id"


def _issue(identifier: str, sort_order: float) -> dict:
    return {
        "id": f"id-{identifier}",
        "identifier": identifier,
        "title": f"Title for {identifier}",
        "description": f"Body for {identifier}",
        "sortOrder": sort_order,
        "state": {"type": "unstarted", "name": "Todo"},
        "labels": [f"repo:{_TEST_REPO_NAME}"],
    }


def _stub_repos(repo_path: Path) -> repos.Repos:
    return repos.Repos(mapping={_TEST_REPO_NAME: repo_path})


def _init_repo(repo: Path) -> None:
    subprocess.run(["git", "init", "-b", "main"], cwd=repo, check=True, capture_output=True)
    subprocess.run(
        ["git", "config", "user.email", "test@example.com"], cwd=repo, check=True
    )
    subprocess.run(["git", "config", "user.name", "Test"], cwd=repo, check=True)
    (repo / "README.md").write_text("seed\n")
    subprocess.run(["git", "add", "."], cwd=repo, check=True, capture_output=True)
    subprocess.run(
        ["git", "commit", "-m", "seed"], cwd=repo, check=True, capture_output=True
    )


def _noop_claude(tmp_path: Path) -> Path:
    """``claude -p`` stand-in that exits cleanly without flipping Linear.

    The orchestrator's not-Done branch then halts, which is the post-spawn
    shape these tests want — the worktree is preserved, the halt is
    recorded, and the next re-run drives the resume path.
    """
    script = tmp_path / "noop-claude.sh"
    script.write_text("#!/bin/sh\nexit 0\n")
    script.chmod(0o755)
    return script


def _seed_runlog_entry(
    runs_dir: Path,
    *,
    cycle_id: str,
    identifier: str,
    final_state: str,
    suffix: str,
) -> Path:
    """Write a minimal run-log file with one entry for ``identifier``.

    Mirrors the on-disk schema enough for ``_resume_attempts`` to read it:
    only ``cycle_id`` + ``entries[].issue_identifier`` + ``final_linear_state``
    are inspected. Filename uses ``<cycle_id>-<suffix>.json`` so the cycle's
    glob picks it up.
    """
    runs_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    path = runs_dir / f"{cycle_id}-{suffix}.json"
    path.write_text(
        json.dumps(
            {
                "cycle_id": cycle_id,
                "cycle_duration_seconds": 0.0,
                "cycle_cost_usd": 0.0,
                "cycle_tokens_cumulative": 0,
                "cycle_halt_reason": None,
                "entries": [
                    {
                        "issue_identifier": identifier,
                        "started_at": "2026-05-28T10:00:00+00:00",
                        "finished_at": "2026-05-28T10:01:00+00:00",
                        "exit_code": -1,
                        "final_linear_state": final_state,
                        "worktree_path": "/tmp/stub",
                        "halt_reason": (
                            None
                            if final_state == "Done"
                            else f"Halt: {identifier} (final state: {final_state})"
                        ),
                        "duration_seconds": 60.0,
                        "model": None,
                        "usage": None,
                        "cost_usd": None,
                        "num_turns": None,
                        "session_id": None,
                        "is_error": None,
                    }
                ],
            }
        )
        + "\n"
    )
    return path


def _patch_linear(
    monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch,
    raw_issues: list[dict],
    *,
    set_state_calls: list[tuple[str, str]],
) -> None:
    """Common Linear-side substitutions for these tests.

    ``current_cycle_id`` returns the fixed test cycle id, ``pending_issues``
    serves the planned order, and ``set_state`` records calls (so a test
    can assert it was — or was not — invoked). ``get_issue`` returns the
    original issue verbatim, which makes a no-op claude script look like
    a not-Done halt to the orchestrator.
    """
    issues_by_id = {i["id"]: i for i in raw_issues}

    def fake_set_state(issue_id: str, state_name: str) -> None:
        set_state_calls.append((issue_id, state_name))

    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "current_cycle_id", lambda: _CYCLE_ID)
    monkeypatch.setattr(
        linear, "pending_issues", lambda cycle_id: linear._plan(raw_issues)
    )
    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "set_state", fake_set_state)
    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "get_issue", lambda issue_id: issues_by_id[issue_id])


def test_resume_reuses_preserved_worktree_and_signals_resumed_prompt(
    tmp_path: Path, monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch
) -> None:
    """Pre-existing worktree on disk is reused; the spawned prompt carries
    ``resumed=True``.

    This is the headline resume contract: a re-run after a halt no longer
    fails on ``git worktree add`` (the previous failure mode), no longer
    requires manual cleanup, and the spawned agent learns it is continuing
    rather than starting fresh — both halves matter, so they are pinned
    in one test.
    """
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)
    monkeypatch.chdir(repo)
    monkeypatch.setenv("HOME", str(tmp_path))

    issue = _issue("ABA-STUCK", sort_order=1.0)

    # Pre-create the preserved worktree as if a prior run halted here.
    preserved = repo / ".worktrees" / issue["identifier"]
    subprocess.run(
        [
            "git",
            "worktree",
            "add",
            "-b",
            issue["identifier"],
            str(preserved),
            "main",
        ],
        cwd=repo,
        check=True,
        capture_output=True,
    )
    sentinel = preserved / "PRESERVED_FILE"
    sentinel.write_text("from the prior halt\n")

    # Pre-seed one halted entry so the orchestrator's resume-cap sees this
    # as a re-run, not a first attempt.
    runs_dir = tmp_path / ".drain-cycle" / "runs"
    _seed_runlog_entry(
        runs_dir,
        cycle_id=_CYCLE_ID,
        identifier=issue["identifier"],
        final_state="Todo",
        suffix="prior",
    )

    build_calls: list[dict] = []
    real_build = prompt.build

    def recording_build(issue_arg, worktree_arg, *, resumed=False):
        build_calls.append(
            {"identifier": issue_arg["identifier"], "resumed": resumed}
        )
        return real_build(issue_arg, worktree_arg, resumed=resumed)

    monkeypatch.setattr(prompt, "build", recording_build)

    set_state_calls: list[tuple[str, str]] = []
    _patch_linear(monkeypatch, [issue], set_state_calls=set_state_calls)

    monkeypatch.setattr(orchestrator, "_CLAUDE_CMD", [str(_noop_claude(tmp_path))])

    exit_code = orchestrator.run(_stub_repos(repo))
    # The no-op claude leaves Linear at Todo, so the orchestrator halts on
    # the not-Done branch — but the load-bearing assertions below are about
    # what happened BEFORE that halt: ensure reused, prompt was resumed.
    assert exit_code == 1

    # The orchestrator called prompt.build with resumed=True — the resume
    # directive will be in the spawned-agent prompt.
    assert build_calls == [{"identifier": issue["identifier"], "resumed": True}]

    # The preserved worktree's sentinel file is still there — ensure did
    # not blow it away or recreate the worktree (which would have wiped
    # the gitignored sentinel).
    assert sentinel.read_text() == "from the prior halt\n"

    # Pre-spawn set_state(In Progress) still fires on a resume — the
    # lifecycle half-owned by the orchestrator does not change because the
    # worktree was reused.
    assert (issue["id"], "In Progress") in set_state_calls


def test_resume_cap_halts_before_any_state_change(
    tmp_path: Path,
    monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch,
    capsys: pytest.CaptureFixture[str],
) -> None:
    """With prior halts at the cap, the orchestrator refuses to spawn.

    The refusal is a no-spawn halt: no ``set_state``, no worktree call,
    no ``claude`` invocation. The halt entry is written so KR1 grading
    sees the refused attempt, and the halt line names the cap so the
    operator knows how to clear it.
    """
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)
    monkeypatch.chdir(repo)
    monkeypatch.setenv("HOME", str(tmp_path))

    stuck = _issue("ABA-STUCK", sort_order=1.0)
    later = _issue("ABA-LATER", sort_order=2.0)

    # Seed two prior halted entries so an ``max_resume_attempts=2`` cap
    # has been reached.
    runs_dir = tmp_path / ".drain-cycle" / "runs"
    for suffix in ("first", "second"):
        _seed_runlog_entry(
            runs_dir,
            cycle_id=_CYCLE_ID,
            identifier=stuck["identifier"],
            final_state="Todo",
            suffix=suffix,
        )

    set_state_calls: list[tuple[str, str]] = []
    _patch_linear(monkeypatch, [stuck, later], set_state_calls=set_state_calls)

    # Claude must never be spawned on the cap-halt path; ``set_state``
    # must never be called either (the cap fires before either).
    forbidden = tmp_path / "forbidden-claude.sh"
    forbidden.write_text("#!/bin/sh\nexit 99\n")
    forbidden.chmod(0o755)
    monkeypatch.setattr(orchestrator, "_CLAUDE_CMD", [str(forbidden)])

    exit_code = orchestrator.run(
        _stub_repos(repo), limits.Limits(max_resume_attempts=2)
    )
    assert exit_code == 1

    # No set_state, no spawned worker. The cap-halt fires before either.
    assert set_state_calls == []
    assert not (repo / ".worktrees" / stuck["identifier"]).exists()

    # Single fresh run-log file (the current run) plus the two pre-seeded
    # files. The current one holds exactly one entry: the cap-halt.
    log_files = sorted(runs_dir.glob(f"{_CYCLE_ID}-*.json"))
    assert len(log_files) == 3  # two seeded + one current
    # The current run's file is the one not in the seeded suffixes.
    current_payload = next(
        json.loads(p.read_text())
        for p in log_files
        if p.stem not in (f"{_CYCLE_ID}-first", f"{_CYCLE_ID}-second")
    )
    assert len(current_payload["entries"]) == 1
    entry = current_payload["entries"][0]
    assert entry["issue_identifier"] == stuck["identifier"]
    assert entry["final_linear_state"] == stuck["state"]["name"]
    assert "resume-attempt cap reached (2/2)" in entry["halt_reason"]

    # Stderr halt line matches the run-log halt_reason exactly — the same
    # invariant every other halt path holds.
    stderr_lines = capsys.readouterr().err.splitlines()
    (halt_line,) = [line for line in stderr_lines if line.startswith("Halt: ")]
    assert entry["halt_reason"] == halt_line
    assert "max_resume_attempts" in halt_line

    # Second issue never attempted.
    assert all(
        e["issue_identifier"] != later["identifier"]
        for e in current_payload["entries"]
    )


def test_resume_cap_none_is_unbounded(
    tmp_path: Path, monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch
) -> None:
    """``max_resume_attempts=None`` lets a perma-stuck issue keep retrying.

    The operator opts into that risk explicitly by setting the cap to
    ``null`` in ``limits.yml``. Five prior halts must not block the
    sixth attempt.
    """
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)
    monkeypatch.chdir(repo)
    monkeypatch.setenv("HOME", str(tmp_path))

    issue = _issue("ABA-STUCK", sort_order=1.0)

    runs_dir = tmp_path / ".drain-cycle" / "runs"
    for i in range(5):
        _seed_runlog_entry(
            runs_dir,
            cycle_id=_CYCLE_ID,
            identifier=issue["identifier"],
            final_state="Todo",
            suffix=f"halt{i}",
        )

    set_state_calls: list[tuple[str, str]] = []
    _patch_linear(monkeypatch, [issue], set_state_calls=set_state_calls)

    monkeypatch.setattr(orchestrator, "_CLAUDE_CMD", [str(_noop_claude(tmp_path))])

    # max_resume_attempts=None — the cap is off, the orchestrator must
    # still attempt the issue (and the no-op claude then halts on not-Done
    # like any first attempt, which is fine for this test's purpose).
    exit_code = orchestrator.run(
        _stub_repos(repo), limits.Limits(max_resume_attempts=None)
    )
    assert exit_code == 1
    # The pre-spawn In Progress transition fired — proves the cap did NOT
    # short-circuit ahead of it.
    assert (issue["id"], "In Progress") in set_state_calls


def test_resume_cap_does_not_count_done_entries(
    tmp_path: Path, monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch
) -> None:
    """Prior Done entries are excluded from the cap tally.

    Without this, a successfully drained issue in a prior run would
    consume the resume budget of a later, unrelated re-run against the
    same cycle — pinning the cap to ``Done`` entries instead of halts.
    """
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)
    monkeypatch.chdir(repo)
    monkeypatch.setenv("HOME", str(tmp_path))

    issue = _issue("ABA-STUCK", sort_order=1.0)
    runs_dir = tmp_path / ".drain-cycle" / "runs"

    # Five prior Done entries plus one prior halt. With max_resume_attempts=2
    # the cap is NOT reached (1 < 2) — the run must proceed.
    for i in range(5):
        _seed_runlog_entry(
            runs_dir,
            cycle_id=_CYCLE_ID,
            identifier=issue["identifier"],
            final_state="Done",
            suffix=f"done{i}",
        )
    _seed_runlog_entry(
        runs_dir,
        cycle_id=_CYCLE_ID,
        identifier=issue["identifier"],
        final_state="Todo",
        suffix="halt",
    )

    set_state_calls: list[tuple[str, str]] = []
    _patch_linear(monkeypatch, [issue], set_state_calls=set_state_calls)

    monkeypatch.setattr(orchestrator, "_CLAUDE_CMD", [str(_noop_claude(tmp_path))])

    exit_code = orchestrator.run(
        _stub_repos(repo), limits.Limits(max_resume_attempts=2)

lines: Design rationale for `drain-cycle`. Read this before making architectural changes — `AGENTS.md` points here.
-Thirteen decisions are recorded so a future reader doesn't have to reverse-engineer them from the code. ADRs would be heavier than this tool needs.
+Fourteen decisions are recorded so a future reader doesn't have to reverse-engineer them from the code. ADRs would be heavier than this tool needs.
## 1. The spawned agent updates Linear itself

input.old_string:
- **Parallelism.** Issues run one at a time. The Linear cycle is the unit; intra-cycle parallelism adds resource contention and serialises poorly with the agent-self-update pattern (two agents racing to mark different issues Done is fine, but two agents racing on overlapping files is not).
- **Retry.** A halted issue is not retried automatically. The operator inspects the worktree and decides — fix, redo manually, or descope.
- **Cross-cycle scheduling.** One cycle per invocation. Chaining cycles is an operator concern.
input.new_string:
- **Parallelism.** Issues run one at a time. The Linear cycle is the unit; intra-cycle parallelism adds resource contention and serialises poorly with the agent-self-update pattern (two agents racing to mark different issues Done is fine, but two agents racing on overlapping files is not).
- **Retry.** Superseded by §14 — a halted issue is now resumed automatically on re-run by reusing its preserved worktree, bounded by ``max_resume_attempts``. The operator-owned manual path (inspect, fix, redo, descope) still applies; the change is that the default `drain-cycle` re-run no longer fails on a leftover worktree.
- **Cross-cycle scheduling.** One cycle per invocation. Chaining cycles is an operator concern.

## 9. Resource guardrails: a native cost belt and orchestrator token/time suspenders

input.old_string: - *A span per private helper (e.g. `_plan`, `link_project_config`).* Rejected as over-instrumentation: those are fast, pure, and not independently aggregable. Interesting, failure-prone, or aggregable operations get spans; the rest stay as attributes on their parent.
input.new_string:
- *A span per private helper (e.g. `_plan`, `link_project_config`).* Rejected as over-instrumentation: those are fast, pure, and not independently aggregable. Interesting, failure-prone, or aggregable operations get spans; the rest stay as attributes on their parent.

## 14. Halted issues resume on re-run by reusing the preserved worktree

Before this, a halted issue's worktree was preserved on disk for the operator to inspect, but a re-run of `drain-cycle` against the same cycle would call `git worktree add` on the same path and fail opaquely — every halt required a manual `rm -rf .worktrees/<id>` plus `git worktree prune` plus `git branch -D <id>` before the next run could even reach the spawn. That friction punished the exact workflow the tool exists for: "halt, inspect, re-run, drain the rest."

**Decision.** `worktree.ensure` replaces `worktree.add` in the orchestrator's pre-spawn path: if a worktree is already registered at `repo/.worktrees/<identifier>`, it is reused as-is (no mutating git command runs, so a dirty index, staged or untracked files, and the gitignored config symlinks all survive untouched); otherwise it falls through to `add` exactly as before. The handle returned carries a `resumed: bool` flag that the orchestrator threads into `prompt.build(..., resumed=…)`. When true, the spawned prompt prepends a "Resuming issue …" directive that tells the agent to run `git log --oneline main..HEAD` and `git status` first to read what is already done before continuing — so the agent does not restart from scratch and clobber the prior work.

**Bounded by `limits.max_resume_attempts`.** A perma-stuck issue would otherwise consume an attempt on every re-run forever. The orchestrator counts prior halted attempts for the issue across the cycle's run-log files (entries with a non-Done `final_linear_state` matching the identifier) and refuses to spawn once the count reaches the cap. The refusal is a no-spawn halt: no Linear `set_state`, no worktree manipulation, no worker invocation; a halt entry is still written so KR1 grading sees the refused attempt and the halt line names the cap so the operator knows how to clear it (raise the cap, clear prior runs, or finish by hand). The default is 3 (one fresh + three resumes); `null` removes the cap entirely. `max_resume_attempts` is a *policy* cap, not a runtime guardrail in the §9 sense — no `Breach` is raised, the check is purely pre-spawn against the run-log history, and the field validation is integer-only (`1.5` would be incoherent on a count).

**Why the cap-halt fires before `worktree.ensure` and `set_state`.** The point of refusing is to leave the issue exactly where it was so the operator can intervene without untangling a half-done re-run. Calling `worktree.ensure` first would be harmless (`ensure` is read-only on the worktree-already-registered branch), but `set_state(In Progress)` would not — a refused attempt that nevertheless flipped Linear to In Progress would silently exclude the issue from the next `pending_issues` query and the cycle would stall invisibly. The order is: resolve repo → resume-cap check → `worktree.ensure` → `link_project_config` → `set_state` → spawn. Each step is a pure no-op until the one before it succeeds.

**Why "resumed" is a prompt directive, not a worker flag.** The worker is just a `claude -p` subprocess; the only contract surface between orchestrator and agent is the prompt string. Adding a CLI flag for "resumed" to the worker would still resolve to "include this paragraph in the system context," and the prompt is already that context. The four-segment ordering in `prompt.py` (title → body → preamble → tail) is load-bearing; the resume directive inserts as the first line of the preamble (after the `---` separator, before "Execution instructions:") so the agent reads it ahead of the procedure while `_TAIL` keeps the last-line position the ordering reserves for it.

**Run-log entries treat resume halts the same as first-attempt halts.** A halt entry written on a resumed run has identical shape to one from a fresh attempt — same `final_linear_state`, same `worktree_path`, same `halt_reason` template via `_halt_message`. This is deliberate: KR1 grading and the cap-counting helper both read `final_linear_state` per entry, and giving resumed halts a different shape would split the schema into two cases for no benefit. The cap-halt entry uses `exit_code=-1` (the no-spawn sentinel, like the resolution and setup-failure halts in §8/§5) and carries the cap-specific message in `halt_reason` so the operator can grep for it.

**This supersedes the `rerun-after-halt-detect-cleanly` plan (`docs/tasks/`).** That earlier doc proposed a `PriorArtefactsExist` exception that would convert the leftover worktree into a clean `Halt:` line — same friction, prettier error. The resume path eliminates the friction entirely: the operator's mental model is "re-run drains the rest," and `drain-cycle` now matches it. The task doc carries a supersession note pointing here.

**Alternatives considered.**

- *Auto-delete the preserved worktree on re-run.* Rejected. The worktree exists because §3 chose preservation-on-halt for inspectability (US-B). Deleting it on re-run means the operator's evidence is gone the moment they re-run, which is the worst-of-both: they cannot inspect (deleted) but also cannot resume (fresh worktree, no prior work). Preservation + resume is the only combination that lets the operator both inspect *and* re-run.
- *Refuse to re-run while a halted worktree exists, force `drain-cycle clean <id>` first.* Rejected as scope creep — a new subcommand plus a new gating rule, both to enforce the workflow `drain-cycle` already trains. The mental model "halt, inspect, re-run" is the one operators have; a forced clean step adds friction without adding safety (the operator could already have re-run after deletion in the old model).
- *Resume by replaying the agent's last few turns from the worker stream-json log.* Rejected. The transcript is keyed by session and lives outside the worktree (§8); reconstructing context from it duplicates what `git log main..HEAD` and `git status` already tell the agent at the start of a resume. Two sources of truth where one suffices.
- *Resume by passing `--continue` or `--resume <session-id>` to `claude -p` instead of changing the prompt.* Rejected because a halted worker may have died mid-turn with no clean session boundary; the next worker is a fresh `claude -p` against a worktree that already has commits. The prompt directive is the right level of abstraction: "this worktree carries prior committed work; read it before continuing." The mechanism is the same whether the prior session lived for one turn or one hour.
- *Cap-halt resets when the operator manually marks the issue Done in Linear.* Considered — the cap counts non-Done entries, so a manual Done in Linear does NOT directly reset the cap, because the prior halted entries in the run log still carry their non-Done `final_linear_state`. The operator clears the cap by raising it, deleting the relevant run-log files, or simply not re-running. This is fine: the cap exists to prevent infinite resume loops, not to track Linear state — Linear state can flip independently of the on-disk history.

lines: - *Always-on tracing.* Rejected: it would force an exporter and an egress dependency on an operator who hasn't asked for it, and fail noisily (or silently retry) when Honeycomb is unreachable. Opt-in keeps the default path dependency-free.

- *Metrics and logs alongside traces.* Out of scope. The run log already covers durable per-issue accounting; traces add the causal/nesting view. A metrics layer can be added later if cost-rate alerting is wanted (see the otel-instrumentation layering guidance).

- *A span per private helper (e.g. `_plan`, `link_project_config`).* Rejected as over-instrumentation: those are fast, pure, and not independently aggregable. Interesting, failure-prone, or aggregable operations get spans; the rest stay as attributes on their parent.

content.subject: Move issue to In Progress in Linear
content.description: Transition ABA-299 to In Progress (and ensure assigned to current cycle).

content.subject: PR1: worktree.ensure primitive + tests
content.description: Add WorktreeHandle, _is_registered_worktree, and ensure() to worktree.py; add reuse + leftover tests. Keep add() unchanged. gt create, run pytest, gt submit, STOP for approval.

content.subject: PR2: prompt resumed variant
content.description: Add resumed=False kwarg to prompt.build; prepend resume directive when True; keep _TAIL last; tests updated. gt create on top of PR1, pytest, gt submit, STOP for approval.

content.subject: PR3: limits max_resume_attempts knob
content.description: Add max_resume_attempts to Limits dataclass (default 3, None=off), validate via _coerce, with tests. gt create on top of PR2, pytest, gt submit, STOP for approval.

content.subject: PR4: orchestrator wiring + docs
content.description: Swap add→ensure, thread resumed flag, add _resume_attempts + cap guard before set_state. Integration tests in test_orchestrator_resume.py. Docs: §14 design-decisions, README, supersession note on rerun-after-halt-detect-cleanly.md. Full pytest, gt submit, STOP for approval.

aiTitle: Resume halted issue on re-run with preserved worktree

content.text: Now updating the README to mention resume + the cap, then adding the supersession note.

input.old_string: After the run, one JSON log per invocation is at `~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>-<run-timestamp>.json`. On halt, the worktree at `<target-repo>/.worktrees/<issue-identifier>/` is preserved for inspection; on success it's removed.
input.new_string:
After the run, one JSON log per invocation is at `~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>-<run-timestamp>.json`. On halt, the worktree at `<target-repo>/.worktrees/<issue-identifier>/` is preserved for inspection; on success it's removed.

A subsequent `drain-cycle` against the same cycle picks the halted issue back up automatically: the preserved worktree is reused (no `git worktree` collision, no manual cleanup), and the spawned agent is told it is resuming so it reads the committed work in the worktree before continuing. After `max_resume_attempts` halts (default `3`; configurable in `~/.drain-cycle/limits.yml` — see [Limits](#limits)), the orchestrator refuses to spawn for that issue and writes a cap-halt entry, so a perma-stuck issue cannot consume budget forever. Set the cap to `null` to opt out of the cap entirely. See [`docs/design-decisions.md`](docs/design-decisions.md) §14.

After the run, one JSON log per invocation is at `~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>-<run-timestamp>.json`. On halt, the worktree at `<target-repo>/.worktrees/<issue-identifier>/` is preserved for inspection; on success it's removed.

A subsequent `drain-cycle` against the same cycle picks the halted issue back up automatically: the preserved worktree is reused (no `git worktree` collision, no manual cleanup), and the spawned agent is told it is resuming so it reads the committed work in the worktree before continuing. After `max_resume_attempts` halts (default `3`; configurable in `~/.drain-cycle/limits.yml` — see [Limits](#limits)), the orchestrator refuses to spawn for that issue and writes a cap-halt entry, so a perma-stuck issue cannot consume budget forever. Set the cap to `null` to opt out of the cap entirely. See [`docs/design-decisions.md`](docs/design-decisions.md) §14.

# Example ~/.drain-cycle/limits.yml
#
# Resource guardrails for drain-cycle workers. This file is OPTIONAL: with no
# limits.yml, the baked-in defaults below are all live. Copy to
# ~/.drain-cycle/limits.yml and edit only the caps you want to change.
#
# Precedence: baked-in default -> this file. A key you omit keeps its default;
# a number overrides it; `null` turns that guardrail OFF. Every value must be a
# positive number or null — a typo (0, a negative, a word) halts startup rather
# than silently disabling a cap you believed was active.
#
# Token count is the primary guardrail (a subscription user pays in tokens);
# cost rides alongside via claude's own --max-budget-usd. Per-issue tokens and
# time are enforced by killing the worker mid-run; the cycle caps stop the run
# between issues when their running total is breached.
#
# Defaults (shown explicitly here — delete any line to keep its default):
per_issue_tokens: 8000000     # kill a worker past this many cumulative tokens
per_issue_seconds: 1200       # kill a worker past 20 minutes of wall-clock
per_issue_cost_usd: 15        # passed to claude as --max-budget-usd
cycle_tokens: 30000000        # stop the cycle past this many cumulative tokens
cycle_seconds: 5400           # stop the cycle past 90 minutes of wall-clock
cycle_cost_usd: 60            # stop the cycle past this much total spend

# To disable a guardrail, set it to null, e.g.:
#   per_issue_cost_usd: null

#   per_issue_cost_usd: null

# Resume policy (not a runtime guardrail — see design-decisions.md §14):
max_resume_attempts: 3        # respawn a halted issue up to this many times
                              # across re-runs before refusing further attempts.
                              # Integer-only; counted from non-Done run-log
                              # entries for the issue across this cycle's runs.

# To disable a guardrail, set it to null, e.g.:
#   per_issue_cost_usd: null
#   max_resume_attempts: null   # never refuse a re-run of a perma-stuck issue

input.new_string:
# Tasks: rerun-after-halt-detect-cleanly

> **Superseded by `docs/design-decisions.md` §14 (halted issues resume on re-run).**
>
> This plan proposed turning the leftover-worktree failure into a clean `Halt:` line by raising a `PriorArtefactsExist` exception before `git worktree add`. The shipped design eliminates the failure entirely: `worktree.ensure` reuses the preserved worktree instead of failing on it, and the spawned agent is told it is resuming so it reads the committed work before continuing. The friction this doc was written to soften no longer exists. See §14 for the chosen design and its alternatives section for why the detect-and-halt-cleanly path was rejected.
>
> Kept on disk so the design history is traceable; do not act on the task list below.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Tasks: rerun-after-halt-detect-cleanly

Design doc: ../design-decisions.md (§ "Out of scope" — retry is explicitly operator-owned, so this work is detect-and-emit-halt-UX, not auto-cleanup)
Linear issue: [ABA-231 — git worktree add fails opaquely when rerunning a halted cycle](https://linear.app/ababushkin/issue/ABA-231); ops-slot work (no parent user story; US-B is closed)
Last updated: 2026-05-22

## Surfaced via

Whole-codebase `/code-review-and-quality` audit on 2026-05-22 (post-commit `13d9f70`). Two findings filed; #1 (`first_pending_issue` dead code) landed as commit `83876f7`. This file plans finding #2.

The review's verbatim framing: on halt, drain-cycle preserves `.worktrees/<id>/` and branch `<id>` for inspection (intentional, US-B). On rerun, the orchestrator re-picks the same first issue and calls `git worktree add -b <id> ...` which fails because both exist; `subprocess.run(check=True)` raises `CalledProcessError` and the operator gets a Python traceback rather than the spec-shaped `Halt: ...` UX they were trained to expect from US-B.

## Confirmed inputs

- **US-B is closed.** ABA-195 / Task 3 (ABA-214) shipped the live acceptance smoke on 2026-05-22. This work is a post-acceptance follow-up, not a US-B re-open. `rules/linear-workflow.md` classifies it as ops-slot work (< 5 issues, not goal-shaped).
- **`_halt_message` helper is in place.** `drain_cycle/orchestrator.py` already has the single-source-of-truth halt-line helper used by both stderr `print` and `runlog.append_entry(halt_reason=...)` (ABA-212/213). The new branch extends/reuses it rather than parallel-stringing the format.
- **Run-log is per-invocation.** Since ABA-230 (commit `bb4fd91`), each invocation writes its own `<cycle-id>-<run-timestamp>.json`, so the rerun-detect-halt entry will land in a *new* file, not clobber the prior halt's file. Test must glob `~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>-*.json` rather than expect a deterministic filename.
- **No auto-cleanup.** `docs/design-decisions.md` §"Out of scope" pins this: "A halted issue is not retried automatically. The operator inspects the worktree and decides — fix, redo manually, or descope." Detect-and-fail-cleanly preserves that contract; the preserved worktree dir and branch are left exactly as they were on the prior halt.
- **Detection must cover both halves.** Either the worktree dir or the branch can be present alone after a partial manual cleanup. The pre-check must flag *either* condition, not require both.

## Design decision pinned upfront

**Does the rerun-detect-and-halt case produce a run-log entry?** → **Yes — Option A.**

Rationale: today's invariant is "one entry per attempted issue" and downstream KR1 grading reads `final_linear_state` per entry. If the operator reruns a halted cycle three times without manual cleanup, all three attempts should be visible in the run-log so KR1 sees the repeated-halt pattern. Option B (stderr-only) would make repeat-halt invisible to grading.

Cost of Option A: ~6 lines of orchestrator code to construct the entry with cached `issue["state"]["name"]`, 0-second duration, and an `exit_code` sentinel for the no-spawn case (see Open Questions for sentinel choice).

## Task list

### Task 1 — Detect prior-halt artefacts and emit spec-shaped halt + run-log entry
**Linear:** [ABA-231](https://linear.app/ababushkin/issue/ABA-231) (ops-slot, no parent user story)
**Description:** Add a pre-check to `drain_cycle/worktree.add()` that raises a new exception (e.g. `PriorArtefactsExist`) carrying the worktree path *before* invoking `git worktree add`, when *either* `.worktrees/<id>/` exists or branch `<id>` exists. `drain_cycle/orchestrator.run()` catches it before calling `linear.set_state(..., "In Progress")` (the orchestrator must not transition the issue when no spawn will follow), constructs a halt line via the existing `_halt_message` helper (extended to a sibling variant or with an optional reason argument — same `Halt:` token prefix so the operator's grep anchor remains the single scannable line), writes a run-log entry with `issue_identifier`, cached `issue["state"]["name"]` as `final_linear_state`, `started_at == finished_at` (0-second duration), `exit_code` sentinel for the no-spawn case, and `halt_reason` equal to the stderr line, prints the halt line on stderr, and returns 1.
**Done when:** a new integration test (new file `tests/test_orchestrator_rerun_halt.py` or extension of `tests/test_orchestrator_halt.py`) asserts:
  (a) the orchestrator process exits non-zero;
  (b) exactly one stderr line begins with `Halt: ` and contains the issue identifier and the preserved worktree path;
  (c) globbing `~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>-*.json` (per ABA-230's per-run filename format) returns exactly one file, whose payload has exactly one entry for the picked issue, with `halt_reason` equal to the stderr line, `final_linear_state` equal to the cached `Todo` state, and `cycle_duration_seconds == 0.0`;
  (d) `linear.set_state` was NOT called for the picked issue (no Todo→In Progress transition on the no-spawn branch);
  (e) the second issue in the cycle was not touched (no worktree created, no `set_state`, no run-log entry);
  (f) the pre-existing worktree dir and branch are left in place (no auto-cleanup);
  (g) full `pytest` remains green;
  AND parametrising the test for the two pre-seeded states proves both halves of the detector:
    (g1) worktree dir present + branch present (the post-halt steady state);
    (g2) worktree dir present + branch absent (operator partial cleanup);
    (g3) worktree dir absent + branch present (operator partial cleanup).
**Dependencies:** none. US-B's machinery (`_halt_message`, the `halt_reason` field on run-log entries, the `Halt:` stderr-line shape) is already in place. No coupling to in-flight work.

## Out of scope (deliberate)

- **Auto-cleanup of preserved worktrees or branches.** Operator owns retry per `docs/design-decisions.md`.
- **Changes to the agent prompt.** Spawn-time contract is unaffected; this branch fires before any spawn.
- **Changes to the agent-self-updates-Linear contract.** No Linear state transition happens on the rerun-detect branch.
- **Recovery helper / drain-cycle subcommand.** A future "drain-cycle clean <id>" helper might be useful, but is not in this task.

## Open questions

- **Halt-line wording** for the rerun-detect case. Working text: `Halt: <id> (prior-halt artefacts at <worktree-path>) — clean up before retry`. The `Halt:` prefix + issue identifier + worktree path are the load-bearing invariants; exact prose is decided during implementation, no separate owner.
- **`exit_code` sentinel** in the run-log entry for the no-spawn case. Working choice: `-1`. Alternatives: `null` (requires `runlog` schema change to allow `int | None`, plus a docstring update), or `0` (collides with successful-Done semantics — rejected). Resolution: during implementation; the test's required-keys + per-field-type assertions in `tests/test_runlog.py` will catch any choice the current schema doesn't already allow.

lines: +> This plan proposed turning the leftover-worktree failure into a clean `Halt:` line by raising a `PriorArtefactsExist` exception before `git worktree add`. The shipped design eliminates the failure entirely: `worktree.ensure` reuses the preserved worktree instead of failing on it, and the spawned agent is told it is resuming so it reads the committed work before continuing. The friction this doc was written to soften no longer exists. See §14 for the chosen design and its alternatives section for why the detect-and-halt-cleanly path was rejected.
+> Kept on disk so the design history is traceable; do not act on the task list below.

The shipped design eliminates the failure entirely: `worktree.ensure` reuses the preserved worktree instead of failing on it, and the spawned agent is told it is resuming so it reads the committed work before continuing. The friction this doc was written to soften no longer exists. See §14 for the chosen design and its alternatives section for why the detect-and-halt-cleanly path was rejected.
